methods.description: Process for separating zircon grains from granitoid samples, preparing them for analysis, performing cathodoluminescence imaging and U-Pb isotopic analysis using FE-EPMA and LA-MC-ICP-MS, correcting data using standards, selecting concordant data, and calculating concordia ages using Isoplot software.

steps.description: Zircon grains were separated from the granitoid samples by crushing, sieving, panning, magnetic separation and heavy liquid techniques.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate zircon grains from rock samples using physical and chemical methods.

steps.description: The zircon grains were mounted in resin (SpeciFix, Struers ApS, Denmark)
steps.searchable_summary: Mount separated zircon grains in resin for analysis.

steps.description: and then used for cathodoluminescence (CL) observation
steps.searchable_summary: Acquire cathodoluminescence images of mounted zircon grains.
inputs: Field-emission electron probe microanalyzer (FE-EPMA, JEOL JXA-8530F)

steps.description: and U-Pb isotopic analysis using ... multiple collector inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (Neptune-Plus, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bremen, Germany) with an excimer laser system (Analyte G2; Photon Machines, Redmond, WA, USA) (LA-MC-ICP-MS), respectively, at Tono Geoscience Center, JAEA.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure U-Pb isotopic ratios of zircon grains using LA-MC-ICP-MS.

steps.description: Elemental fractionation and instrumental mass bias on 206 Pb/ 238 U, 207 Pb/ 206 Pb ratios were corrected using the measured isotope ratio of the 91,500 zircon (Wiedenbeck et al. 1995) as primary standard with a standard-sample bracketing approach.
steps.searchable_summary: Correct raw U-Pb isotopic data for fractionation and bias using primary standard and standard-sample bracketing.

steps.description: The OD-3 zircon (Iwano et al., 2013) was used as secondary standard material for age quality control.
steps.searchable_summary: Use secondary standard data to assess the quality of age measurements.

steps.description: The analytical spots of unknowns were determined based on the growth structures of zircons observed by CL images (Fig. 2); the structures show oscillatory zoning patterns (e.g., 1-TNG20-05-01, 1-TNG20-09-04) or homogeneous textures (e.g., 1-TNG20-03-13, TNG20-10-08).
steps.searchable_summary: Select specific analytical spots on zircon grains based on CL images and growth structures.
outputs: Coordinates or locations of analytical spots

steps.description: In this paper, the data were defined as 'concordant' when overlapping the concordia curve on a concordia diagram within error range of 1sigma.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter U-Pb dating data to select 'concordant' analyses based on 1-sigma error relative to the concordia curve.

steps.description: Isoplot software ver. 4.15 (Ludwig 2012) was used to produce the concordia diagrams
steps.searchable_summary: Generate concordia diagrams from U-Pb dating data using Isoplot software.

steps.description: and the concordia ages (2 sigma-weighted mean age of 207 Pb/ 235 U and 206 Pb/ 238 U isotopes; Ludwig, 1998) using the 'concordant' data ( Fig. 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate concordia ages (2-sigma weighted mean) from concordant U-Pb isotopic data using Isoplot software.

steps.description: the results are summarized in Table 1 (for the details, see also Additional file 2: Table S2).
steps.searchable_summary: Compile and summarize U-Pb dating results in tables.

methods.description: Describes the geological setting of the study area, including location, topography, climate, glacial history, and lithology. It also details the collection of specific rock samples (granitoids and quartzdiorite) from different bodies within the area for subsequent dating.

steps.description: The Tanigawa-dake area is located on the back-arc side of the Northeast Japan Arc. Mt. Tanigawa-dake (1977 m high) is a non-volcanic mountain surrounded by Quaternary volcanoes, such as, Mt. Naeba, Mt. Iiji and Mt. Hotaka (Fig. 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Describe the geographical and geological location of the study area.
outputs: Text description of study area context

steps.description: Coastal areas of the Sea of Japan, including the Tanigawa-dake area, is one of the heaviest snow areas in the world (e.g., Ueda et al. 2015). In addition, glacial landforms formed at the last glacial period are distributed in the Tanigawa-dake and adjacent mountains (Koaze 2002).
steps.searchable_summary: Describe the physical environment (climate, glaciation) of the study area.

steps.description: Lithology of the Tanigawa-dake area consists mainly of Cretaceous to Paleogene granitoids, late Miocene to Pliocene granitoids, and Miocene to Quaternary volcanic rocks (Sato 2016 : Fig. 1). The late Miocene to Pliocene granites are called the Tanigawa-dake granites, being subdivided into three bodies, i.e., the Makihata body, the Tanigawa body, and the Akayu body, from east to west (Chihara et al. 1981). The Makihata body and the Tanigawa body are exposed on the eastern and western sides of Mt. Tanigawa-dake, respectively. The Akayu body is distributed on the southern side of Mt. Naeba. The late Cretaceous plutonic rock is called the Minakami quartzdiorite (Kubo et al. 2013;Sato 2016) which is distributed to the southeast of Mt. Tanigawadake.
steps.searchable_summary: Describe the rock types and their distribution in the study area.
inputs: Geological maps (Fig. 1, Sato 2016, GSJ web page)
outputs: Text description of lithology and distribution

steps.description: In the previous studies, these rocks were dated based on fission-track dating of zircon and apatite, K-Ar dating of biotite and whole rock, and Rb-Sr dating of whole rock. The biotite K-Ar (3.9-3.1 Ma; Kawano et al 1992;Sato, 2016) and zircon fission-track ages (3.3-2.9 Ma; Ohira et al. 1998;Ohira and Honda 1999) are consistent with each other within error range of 2 sigma regardless of location. Therefore, cooling ages of the Tanigawa-dake granites were spatially uniformed at ~ 250-400 °C. However, thermal histories above the temperature range were not well-known, including the timing of the intrusion of the Tanigawa-dake granites.
steps.searchable_summary: Review existing geochronological data for the study area to identify knowledge gaps.
inputs: Scientific literature on previous dating studies (fission-track, K-Ar, Rb-Sr)

steps.description: In this study, for obtaining clearly intrusive ages of the Tanigawa-dake granites and intruded granites, we collected 4 rock samples: TNG20-03 and TNG20-10 from the Tanigawa body, TNG20-05 from the Makihata body, and TNG20-09 from the Minakami quartzdiorite ( Fig. 1, Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect rock samples from specified geological units and locations for dating.
outputs: Collected rock samples (TNG20-03, TNG20-10, TNG20-05, TNG20-09)

steps.description: Circle points denote sampling localities. Colored circles denote sampling localities in this study. ... Note that the sites of TNG20-10 indicated by a red circle overlap the localities of zircon fission-track and biotite K-Ar ages of the previous researches (Kawano et al. 1992;Ohira et al. 1998;Ohira and Honda 1999;Sato 2016). Similarly, the site of TNG20-09 shown by a navy circle overlap the locality of K-Ar age of the whole rock (Kubo et al. 2013)
steps.searchable_summary: Record and map the locations where samples were collected, noting overlap with previous study sites.
